mnemonics.title: SP and FP Purpose
mnemonics.content: SP Shows Position, FP Fixes Position - SP moves during execution, FP stays fixed for stable access

mnemonics.title: Stack Operations
mnemonics.content: PUSH: Put Data In, Subtract pointer (PUSH = Put + Down) / POP: Get Data Out, Add pointer (POP = Pull + Up)

mnemonics.title: Callee vs Caller Saved
mnemonics.content: Callee Conserved, Caller Can Change - Callee-saved must be preserved, Caller-saved can be overwritten

acronyms.usage: Points to top of stack, tracks where next push/pop occurs
Fixed reference point within stack frame for parameter/variable access
Alternative name for Frame Pointer in x86 architecture
Stores return address during function call (used in ARM)
Holds address of next instruction to execute
Function start code that sets up stack frame (save FP, allocate space)
Function end code that restores stack frame and returns

visualTips.visualization: Imagine stack as a plate dispenser in cafeteria - plates (data) are added/removed from top. x86: SP decreases (grows down), think of stack as descending into memory addresses.
Draw stack frame as a table: [Return Addr][Saved FP][Params][Locals] with FP as anchor pointing to Saved FP, negative offsets for params, positive for locals
Think of stack frames as stacked books - each function call adds a book on top, return removes the top book, revealing caller's book

analogies.analogy: Like a stack of trays in a cafeteria - the tray on top is always accessible (like top register), getting/adding trays is quick (single motion), limited by how many trays physically fit on the counter.
Like a person's desk during a project - you have a workspace (stack frame) with current work (locals), reference materials (parameters), and notes about where you were before (return address).

analogies.analization: SP is like a moving bookmark showing current position in book, FP is like a sticky note marking the start of current chapter - you always know where chapter starts but page changes.
